No more misusing POST for read-only searches!
Press enter or click to view image in full size

As developers, we’ve been using the same HTTP methods for years:

• GET → Fetch data

• POST → Create data

• PUT → Update data

• DELETE → Delete data
But there has always been one problem.
What if you want to fetch data, but your request is too complex to fit into a URL?
For years, we had to use POST even though we weren't creating or updating anything.
Now, that’s changing.
A new HTTP method called QUERY has been introduced, giving developers a better way to perform complex read-only requests.
Let’s understand it in the simplest way possible.

🤔 The Problem with GET
Imagine you’re building an online shopping application.
Users can search for products using:

• Category

• Brand

• Price Range

• Rating

• Color

• Stock Status

• Multiple Tags
With GETYour request might look like this:
GET /products?category=laptop&brand=apple&rating=4&color=silver&price=50000-150000It works…
But what if the user selects 20 filters?
The URL becomes:

• Very long

• Hard to read

• Difficult to maintain

• Sometimes even exceeds URL length limits
That’s where developers started using POST.

😕 Why POST Wasn’t the Right Choice
Instead of putting everything in the URL, developers started sending the filters inside the request body.
Example:
POST /products/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "Laptop",
"brand": "Apple",
"rating": 4,
"price": {
"min": 50000,
"max": 150000
}
}This looks much cleaner.
But there’s one issue.
POST is mainly meant for:

• Creating data

• Updating data

• Performing actions
In this example, we’re only reading data.
Nothing is being created.
Nothing is being modified.
Using POST Here isn't semantically correct.

🎉 Meet the New HTTP Method: QUERY
The new QUERY method solves this problem.
It allows you to:
✅ Send a request body
✅ Fetch data only
✅ Keep the request safe
Example:
QUERY /products
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "Laptop",
"brand": "Apple",
"rating": 4
}The server understands that:
“This request is only asking for data. It won’t change anything.”
That’s exactly what developers have wanted for years.

📊 GET vs POST vs QUERY
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Think of it like this:
GET
“I want data.”
POST
“I want to change something.”
QUERY
“I want data, but I need to send lots of information first.”
Simple.

💡 Real-Life Example
Suppose you’re building an employee management system.
Users can search employees by:

• Department

• Experience

• Skills

• Location

• Salary Range

• Joining Date
With GETThe URL could become huge.
Instead, you can write:
QUERY /employees/search{
"department": "Engineering",
"experience": 5,
"skills": [
"Angular",
"TypeScript"
]
}Much cleaner.
Much easier to maintain.

🌍 Where Can QUERY Be Used?
The new method is useful in many real-world applications.
🛒 E-commerce
Searching for products with many filters.

📊 Analytics Dashboards
Reports often include dozens of filters like date range, region, product type, and customer segment.

🤖 AI Applications
Searching vector databases or sending semantic search requests usually requires large JSON payloads.
QUERY Fits perfectly.

👨‍💻 Enterprise Applications
Applications like HR systems, CRM platforms, hospital management systems, and banking dashboards often need complex search requests.

👍 Benefits of QUERY
Using QUERY Gives several advantages.

• Cleaner API design

• Supports JSON request bodies

• Correct HTTP semantics

• Easier to understand

• Better for complex searches

• Safe to retry because it doesn’t modify data

⚠️ Can You Use It Today?
Not everywhere.
Although QUERY is now an official HTTP method; support is still growing.
Many browsers, frameworks, API gateways, and servers need time to add support.
So don’t be surprised if your favorite framework doesn’t support it yet.
Over time, you’ll likely see more tools adopting it.

🎯 Should You Use QUERY?
If your API performs complex read-only searches, QUERY is a much better choice than using POST.
However, before adopting it in production, make sure your server, framework, API gateway, and client libraries support the new method.
For now, many applications will continue using POST For compatibility.

🎉 Final Thoughts
For years, developers had to choose between:

• A long and messy GET URL

• A technically incorrect POST request
Now we finally have a better option.
QUERY brings together the best parts of both methods:

• The safety of GET

• The flexibility of a request body
It may take some time before it becomes common, but it solves a problem that developers have faced for decades.
I’m excited to see how API frameworks and tools adopt QUERY in the coming years.

💬 What do you think?
Will you start using QUERY once your framework supports it?
Or will you continue using POST for search APIs?
I’d love to hear your thoughts in the comments!
